# Albert Hofman (epidemiologist)

Ara Eve Leo Sal

1x

Albert Hofman is a Dutch clinical epidemiologist renowned for his pioneering contributions to the epidemiology of neurologic and vascular diseases, particularly dementia, stroke, and Alzheimer's disease. <sup>[<sup>[[1]]()</sup>]()</sup> He holds the position of Chair of the Department of Epidemiology and serves as the Stephen B. Kay Family Professor of Public Health and Clinical Epidemiology at the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. <sup>[<sup>[[2]]()</sup>]()</sup> Born in the Netherlands, Hofman earned his MD and PhD degrees and built a distinguished career focused on large-scale cohort studies to uncover determinants of chronic diseases. <sup>[<sup>[[3]]()</sup>]()</sup> From 1988 to 2016, he chaired the Department of Epidemiology at Erasmus Medical Center/Erasmus University Rotterdam, where he also directed the Netherlands Institute for Health Sciences graduate school from 1990 to 2015. <sup>[<sup>[[2]]()</sup>]()</sup> In 2016, he transitioned to Harvard, maintaining a part-time affiliation with Erasmus until his retirement from that role in 2017. <sup>[<sup>[[3]]()</sup>]()</sup> Hofman's research emphasizes the interplay between vascular factors, genetics, and aging in disease etiology, including innovative studies on cerebral hemodynamics, cognitive decline, and structural brain changes in Alzheimer's. <sup>[<sup>[[2]]()</sup>]()</sup> He initiated and leads major cohort studies such as **The Rotterdam Study** , a prospective population-based investigation tracking aging and age-related diseases in over 15,000 participants since 1990, and the **Generation R Study** , which examines prenatal and early-life influences on health outcomes in a multi-ethnic birth cohort of nearly 10,000 children. <sup>[<sup>[[1]]()</sup>]()</sup> These efforts have advanced understanding of disease prevention and informed public health strategies globally. <sup>[<sup>[[1]]()</sup>]()</sup> Among his notable achievements, Hofman is recognized as the most highly cited scientist in the Netherlands, with an extensive publication record in top journals such as _The Lancet_ , _Stroke_ , and _Hypertension_ . <sup>[<sup>[[1]]()</sup>]()</sup> In 2021, he was knighted as a Knight of the Order of the Lion of the Netherlands for his exceptional contributions to science and society. <sup>[<sup>[[1]]()</sup>]()</sup> More recently, in 2024, he received an honorary doctorate from the National and Kapodistrian University of Athens School of Medicine for his leadership in dementia and stroke epidemiology. <sup>[<sup>[[4]]()</sup>]()</sup>

## Early Life and Education

### Early Life

Albert Hofman was born in 1951 in Hardenberg, a small town in the rural province of Overijssel, Netherlands. He completed his secondary education in Hardenberg, earning the HBS-B diploma—a qualification preparing students for higher education—in 1968. Following this, Hofman transitioned to formal medical training at the University of Groningen.

### Medical and Research Training

Hofman earned his medical degree (MD) from the University of Groningen in 1976, completing his formal medical education in the Netherlands. <sup>[<sup>[[5]]()</sup>]()</sup> Prior to graduation, he served as a research fellow in the Department of Community Medicine at the University of Groningen in 1975, gaining early exposure to public health research during his medical studies. <sup>[<sup>[[6]]()</sup>]()</sup> Following his MD, Hofman pursued clinical training through residencies in internal medicine at the Academic Hospitals of Groningen and Leiden in 1977, building practical expertise in patient care and clinical assessment. <sup>[<sup>[[6]]()</sup>]()</sup> In 1982, he completed a research fellowship in the Department of Epidemiology at the Harvard School of Public Health, where his work emphasized advanced epidemiological methods under the supervision of Professor Olli S. Miettinen. <sup>[<sup>[[6]]()</sup>]()</sup> This period of clinical and research training equipped him with essential skills for his subsequent PhD pursuits at Erasmus University Rotterdam. <sup>[<sup>[[5]]()</sup>]()</sup>

### Doctoral Dissertation

Albert Hofman earned his PhD in 1983 from Erasmus University Rotterdam, where he was promoted _cum laude_ under the supervision of Prof. Dr. H.A. Valkenburg, with co-referents Prof. Dr. W.H. Birkenhäger and Prof. Dr. H.K.A. Visser. <sup>[<sup>[[6]]()</sup>]()</sup> The dissertation, titled _Blood Pressure in Childhood: Epidemiological Probes into the Aetiology of High Blood Pressure_ , was defended on April 6, 1983, at 3:45 p.m. in the Senate Hall of the Erasmus University Medical Faculty. Conducted within the Department of Epidemiology at Erasmus University Rotterdam, the work exemplified the institution's emphasis on population-based research to uncover the origins of chronic diseases before clinical manifestations, integrating observational and experimental designs in an open-population setting. The research centered on the distribution, determinants, and tracking of blood pressure (BP) in children aged 5–19, drawing primarily from the prospective Epidemiological Study on Obesity in Zoetermeer (EPOZ) cohort of 3,924 participants (82% response rate) initiated in 1975. Systolic BP increased with age, more steeply in boys after age 13 (reaching 131 mmHg at age 19 versus 120 mmHg in girls), while diastolic BP rose modestly; weight and pulse rate emerged as key determinants, accounting for 47% of systolic and 10% of diastolic variability, with modest familial correlations from parental BP (regression coefficients 0.11–0.13). Persistently elevated BP (>140/90 mmHg across three readings) affected approximately 4% of boys and 0.6% of girls aged 15–19, with tracking correlations of 0.68 for systolic and 0.41 for diastolic over four weeks, and around 0.5 for systolic over 2–3 years. Early-life factors, particularly sodium intake, showed significant influence: a retrospective study of 348 schoolchildren linked higher sodium in drinking water (7.0–7.4 mEq/L versus 1.3 mEq/L) to 1.8–4.0 mmHg elevations in systolic and diastolic BP after adjustments for confounders like age, weight, and family history; a double-blind randomized trial in 476 newborns demonstrated that a low-sodium formula (6.3 mEq/L) reduced systolic BP rise by about 2.1 mmHg at 25 weeks compared to normal-sodium (19.2 mEq/L), with effects persisting and increasing over six months. Methodologically, Hofman innovated by combining cross-sectional analyses with longitudinal follow-up in the EPOZ subset (n=596, with 3–7 annual readings using random-zero sphygmomanometers) and integrating non-invasive haemodynamic assessments via M-mode echocardiography in a case-control of 41 initial hypertensives versus matched normotensives aged 10–19, revealing no hyperkinetic phase but elevated peripheral resistance (26.8 versus 22.8 units) and plasma noradrenaline (336 versus 281 pg/ml). Complementary data from a Boston cohort (n=319, aged 15–19) confirmed no association between mean arterial pressure and cardiac output index (regression coefficient 0.6 mmHg/L/min/m², nonsignificant), underscoring resistance over output as the primary driver of elevated childhood BP. Statistical approaches included stepwise multiple regression for determinants, Pearson correlations for tracking, t-tests for group differences, and adjustments for regression to the mean (e.g., Blomqvist method), enhancing the reliability of longitudinal BP dynamics in pediatric cohorts. These findings challenged the prevailing hyperkinetic hypothesis of hypertension etiology, attributing early elevations to sympathetic overactivity increasing peripheral resistance via noradrenaline and renin, while highlighting modifiable environmental factors like sodium as precursors to adult cardiovascular risk. By proposing BP deviations from genetic "tracks" as key to atherogenesis—especially abrupt early changes accelerating plaque formation—the dissertation advocated for population-level interventions, such as sodium reduction in infancy, over individual screening due to modest predictive power (e.g., only 27–44% of upper-decile children remained high after four years). This early focus on pediatric cohort methodologies laid essential groundwork for Hofman's subsequent advancements in clinical epidemiology.

## Professional Career

### Academic Positions in the Netherlands

Hofman began his academic career at Erasmus University Medical School in Rotterdam, Netherlands, where he was appointed as an assistant professor in 1981. <sup>[<sup>[[6]]()</sup>]()</sup> In this role, he contributed to early teaching and research in clinical epidemiology, laying the groundwork for his subsequent advancements. He was promoted to associate professor in 1984, expanding his involvement in departmental activities and research initiatives. <sup>[<sup>[[6]]()</sup>]()</sup> In 1988, Hofman was elevated to full professor and appointed chair of the Department of Epidemiology at Erasmus Medical Center, a position he maintained until 2016. <sup>[<sup>[[7]]()</sup>]()</sup> Under his leadership, the department grew significantly, emphasizing population-based cohort studies as a core focus; this included his pivotal role in initiating the Rotterdam Study in 1990, which became a cornerstone of the department's research portfolio. <sup>[<sup>[[2]]()</sup>]()</sup> His tenure as chair involved overseeing faculty recruitment, curriculum development, and fostering interdisciplinary collaborations to advance epidemiologic methods in chronic disease research. <sup>[<sup>[[3]]()</sup>]()</sup>

### Leadership and Editorial Roles

Hofman served as Science Director of the graduate school of the Netherlands Institute for Health Sciences (NIHES) from 1990 to 2015. <sup>[<sup>[[2]]()</sup>]()</sup> In this role, he led the institute's efforts to advance postgraduate education in clinical epidemiology and public health research, fostering interdisciplinary training programs that trained hundreds of researchers from around the world. His leadership helped establish NIHES as a key hub for methodological innovation in epidemiology within Europe. <sup>[<sup>[[6]]()</sup>]()</sup> As Editor-in-Chief of the _European Journal of Epidemiology_ since 2003, Hofman has shaped the journal's direction by emphasizing high-quality, peer-reviewed publications on population-based studies and methodological advancements. Under his tenure, the journal broadened its scope to encompass global epidemiological issues while upholding stringent peer review standards to ensure rigorous scientific integrity. This has contributed to the journal's growing influence, with an impact factor rising to 5.9 by 2024. <sup>[<sup>[[8]]()</sup>]()</sup> <sup>[<sup>[[9]]()</sup>]()</sup> Hofman's involvement in international collaborations included his pivotal role in the establishment of the Cohorts for Heart and Aging Research in Genomic Epidemiology (CHARGE) consortium, where the Rotterdam Study—under his principal investigatorship—served as one of the five founding cohorts in 2007. This initiative facilitated large-scale meta-analyses across diverse populations, enhancing collaborative research in cardiovascular and aging-related traits. These leadership positions supported Hofman's broader research initiatives by promoting knowledge dissemination and cross-institutional partnerships. <sup>[<sup>[[8]]()</sup>]()</sup> <sup>[<sup>[[10]]()</sup>]()</sup>

### Transition to Harvard University

In 2016, Albert Hofman transitioned from his long-standing position in the Netherlands to the United States, accepting the role of Chair of the Department of Epidemiology at the Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. This appointment marked a significant shift, building on his extensive leadership experience in Rotterdam to lead one of the world's premier epidemiology departments. <sup>[<sup>[[3]]()</sup>]()</sup> <sup>[<sup>[[2]]()</sup>]()</sup> Upon joining Harvard, Hofman was named the Stephen B. Kay Family Professor of Public Health and Clinical Epidemiology, a title reflecting his expertise in population-based research and clinical applications. In this capacity, he has guided the department's strategic direction, fostering interdisciplinary collaborations that extend the influence of epidemiological methods globally. His move has facilitated the integration of international cohort data into Harvard's research ecosystem, enhancing studies on aging, cardiovascular disease, and public health prevention. <sup>[<sup>[[2]]()</sup>]()</sup> <sup>[<sup>[[1]]()</sup>]()</sup> Post-transition, Hofman adjusted his research focus to emphasize translational epidemiology while maintaining oversight of key Dutch initiatives. As principal investigator, he continues to direct the Rotterdam Study and Generation R Study, ensuring their ongoing contributions to global understanding of disease etiology despite his primary base in Boston. This dual involvement underscores the implications of his relocation for bridging European and American epidemiological efforts, promoting data sharing and methodological advancements across continents. <sup>[<sup>[[1]]()</sup>]()</sup> <sup>[<sup>[[2]]()</sup>]()</sup>

## Research Contributions

### Initiation of the Rotterdam Study

The Rotterdam Study, a landmark prospective population-based cohort study, was initiated by Albert Hofman in 1990 as its principal investigator, focusing on the elderly population in the Ommoord district of Rotterdam, Netherlands, to investigate risk factors for chronic diseases prevalent in later life. <sup>[<sup>[[11]]()</sup>]()</sup> Drawing from his prior research on blood pressure and cardiovascular determinants in aging populations during the 1980s, Hofman designed the study to address the growing need for longitudinal data on multimorbidity in the elderly amid demographic shifts toward an older society. <sup>[<sup>[[12]]()</sup>]()</sup> The initial cohort targeted individuals aged 55 years and older, successfully recruiting 7,983 participants (78% response rate) from 10,215 invitees between January 1990 and September 1993, with baseline assessments encompassing extensive home interviews, clinical examinations, imaging, and biomarker collections to enable comprehensive phenotyping. <sup>[<sup>[[11]]()</sup>]()</sup> The study's core objectives centered on elucidating the incidence, etiology, and preclinical markers of major age-related conditions, including cardiovascular diseases (such as coronary heart disease and stroke), neurological disorders (like dementia and Parkinson's disease), and endocrine conditions (including diabetes and osteoporosis), through repeated examinations every three to four years and continuous morbidity surveillance via linkages to medical records. <sup>[<sup>[[11]]()</sup>]()</sup> From its outset, the design incorporated multidisciplinary assessments, such as vascular imaging, skeletal evaluations, and genetic sampling, laying the groundwork for advanced analyses; notably, brain MRI protocols were introduced in 1995 for a subsample of non-demented participants to detect subclinical pathology, while genome-wide association capabilities were integrated early via DNA repositories, facilitating later high-throughput genotyping. <sup>[<sup>[[11]]()</sup>]()</sup> Early milestones included the completion of baseline data collection by 1993, which provided foundational datasets for initial publications on disease prevalence and risk factors, and the first follow-up cycle from 1993 to 1995, which reinforced the cohort's viability for long-term tracking. <sup>[<sup>[[11]]()</sup>]()</sup> As one of the five founding cohorts of the Cohorts for Heart and Aging Research in Genomic Epidemiology (CHARGE) consortium established in 2007, the Rotterdam Study played a pivotal role in pioneering genome-wide association studies (GWAS) for common complex diseases, enabling meta-analyses across international populations to identify genetic variants influencing traits like QT interval prolongation, lipid levels, and age-related macular degeneration. <sup>[<sup>[[13]]()</sup>]()</sup> This foundational position amplified the study's impact by fostering collaborations that accelerated discoveries in cardiovascular and aging genomics, with the Rotterdam cohort's detailed phenotyping serving as a key asset for validating associations in prospective settings. <sup>[<sup>[[13]]()</sup>]()</sup>

### Development of the Generation R Study

The Generation R Study was initiated in 2002 by Albert Hofman, who served as the principal investigator, in Rotterdam, the Netherlands, with the aim of following a multi-ethnic cohort of pregnant women and their offspring to explore early-life influences on health outcomes. <sup>[<sup>[[1]]()</sup>]()</sup> Enrollment began in April 2002 and continued until January 2006, recruiting 9,778 mothers through prenatal clinics, hospitals, and midwife practices, reflecting the city's diverse urban population where approximately 50% of residents have a non-Western background. <sup>[<sup>[[14]]()</sup>]()</sup> Designed as a population-based prospective cohort study, Generation R tracks participants from fetal life through young adulthood, with comprehensive assessments including prenatal ultrasounds for fetal growth monitoring, postnatal physical examinations, behavioral evaluations, and collection of biological samples for genetic analysis. <sup>[<sup>[[14]]()</sup>]()</sup> The methodology incorporates environmental factors such as maternal diet, lifestyle, and urban exposures alongside advanced imaging techniques and genetic profiling, enabling detailed longitudinal data on child development up to age 25. <sup>[<sup>[[15]]()</sup>]()</sup> A subgroup of over 1,200 mother-child pairs receives intensified monitoring, including repeated neuroimaging and cognitive testing, to deepen insights into subtle developmental trajectories. <sup>[<sup>[[14]]()</sup>]()</sup> The primary objectives center on elucidating the developmental origins of health and disease, particularly how early environmental and genetic interactions contribute to normal and abnormal growth, behavioral patterns, and the emergence of co-morbidities in pediatric populations. By focusing on four key areas—physical growth, cognitive and behavioral development, childhood diseases, and maternal-child healthcare—the study seeks to inform preventive strategies for optimizing health in diverse urban settings. <sup>[<sup>[[14]]()</sup>]()</sup> Key innovations include the integration of emerging technologies for real-time longitudinal tracking, such as digital archiving of ultrasound data and genomic sequencing of cord blood samples, which allow for dynamic modeling of gene-environment interactions over time. <sup>[<sup>[[15]]()</sup>]()</sup> This approach complements the adult-oriented Rotterdam Study by shifting emphasis to preconceptional and early-life determinants. <sup>[<sup>[[14]]()</sup>]()</sup>

### Broader Impacts on Clinical Epidemiology

Hofman's pioneering integration of large-scale magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) into population-based studies, beginning with the Rotterdam Study in 1995, marked a significant advancement in clinical epidemiology by enabling the detection of subclinical brain pathologies in asymptomatic individuals. Early implementations involved axial T2-weighted and high-resolution sequences on hundreds of non-demented participants, evolving to comprehensive protocols on thousands using 1.5 Tesla scanners by the mid-2000s. This approach revealed a high prevalence of incidental findings, such as meningiomas (2.5%) and cerebral aneurysms (2.3%) in over 5,800 older adults, with most remaining stable over up to 9 years of follow-up and rarely requiring intervention beyond monitoring. Furthermore, these imaging efforts uncovered critical interrelations between seemingly unrelated conditions, including silent brain infarcts (affecting nearly 20% of elderly participants and quadrupling stroke risk while doubling dementia odds), white matter lesions (linked to heightened risks of stroke, dementia, and depression), and hippocampal atrophy (preceding cognitive decline by years), thereby shifting focus toward vascular and neurodegenerative pathways in multifactorial diseases. <sup>[<sup>[[11]]()</sup>]()</sup> <sup>[<sup>[[16]]()</sup>]()</sup> Through his leadership in the Cohorts for Heart and Aging Research in Genomic Epidemiology (CHARGE) consortium, which leverages data from the Rotterdam Study alongside other major cohorts, Hofman has substantially contributed to genome-wide association studies (GWAS) identifying genetic variants underlying complex diseases. For Alzheimer's disease, CHARGE meta-analyses have pinpointed loci such as BIN1 (rs744373, odds ratio ~1.2–1.3) and TREM2 (rs75932628), highlighting immune and microglial roles in pathogenesis. In stroke research, variants like NINJ2 (rs12425791 at 12p13, OR 1.30 for ischemic stroke) and FOXC1 (rs12589554 at 6p21 for brain infarcts) have been associated with ischemic subtypes and small vessel disease, informed by Rotterdam's neuroimaging data on silent infarcts and microbleeds. For osteoporosis, contributions to GEFOS efforts identified BMD-influencing loci including RHOBTB1 (rs2707466, reducing lumbar spine BMD by 0.2 SD and raising fracture risk by 20%), advancing understanding of Wnt signaling in bone homeostasis. Cardiovascular applications via CHARGE have revealed over a dozen loci for coronary artery disease, such as CDKN2B-AS1 (rs1333049 at 9p21, OR 1.29), alongside variants for blood pressure (e.g., NPR3 rs12946454, lowering systolic BP by 1 mmHg) and atrial fibrillation, elucidating shared genetic etiologies across these conditions. <sup>[<sup>[[17]]()</sup>]()</sup> Hofman's research has profoundly influenced public health by developing and validating risk prediction models that account for co-morbidities and shared etiologies, facilitating targeted interventions in aging populations. For instance, integrating genetic, imaging, and biomarker data from population cohorts has enhanced models for cardiovascular events, where coronary calcification and carotid plaques independently predict myocardial infarction beyond traditional factors like cholesterol levels. These approaches extend to co-morbidity forecasting, such as linking thyroid dysfunction to increased frailty and cardiovascular mortality (e.g., high free thyroxine associating with 20–30% higher frailty risk), or kidney function decline with stroke and dementia (via cerebral blood flow and white matter integrity metrics). By emphasizing polygenic risk scores and gene-environment interactions, his work supports precision public health strategies, including pharmacogenomics for statin response (e.g., SORT1 variants predicting LDL reduction), ultimately improving lifetime risk assessments for multi-disease burdens in older adults. <sup>[<sup>[[17]]()</sup>]()</sup> <sup>[<sup>[[18]]()</sup>]()</sup> The cumulative impact of Hofman's over 2,000 publications underscores his enduring influence, with key meta-analyses synthesizing data across cohorts to establish subclinical markers as prognostic tools. Notable examples include CHARGE-led efforts on coronary artery calcification, associating variants like PHACTR1 (rs12526453 at 6p24) with vascular tone and incident events, and on carotid intima-media thickness (cIMT), identifying PIM1 (rs6964587 at 7p22, increasing cIMT by 0.006 mm) and SORT1 (rs11169571 at 14q32) as predictors of plaque and atherosclerosis progression. These syntheses, drawing from thousands of participants, have refined etiological models for cardiovascular and related diseases, emphasizing shared pathways like inflammation and lipid metabolism for broader clinical application. <sup>[<sup>[[19]]()</sup>]()</sup> <sup>[<sup>[[17]]()</sup>]()</sup>

## Recognition and Publications

### Honors and Awards

Albert Hofman received his PhD promotion cum laude from Erasmus University Rotterdam in 1983, recognizing the exceptional quality of his dissertation on the epidemiology of blood pressure in childhood. <sup>[<sup>[[6]]()</sup>]()</sup> In 1992, he delivered his inaugural lecture as Professor of Epidemiology at Erasmus University Rotterdam, titled "On patients, populations and the Hippocratic epidemiology," which underscored his commitment to integrating clinical and population-based approaches in epidemiological research. <sup>[<sup>[[6]]()</sup>]()</sup> Hofman's contributions to epidemiology were further acknowledged by his election as a member of the Royal Netherlands Academy of Arts and Sciences in 2002, a prestigious body that honors leading scholars in the Netherlands for their scientific impact. <sup>[<sup>[[6]]()</sup>]()</sup> In 2012, he was awarded the Folksam Prize in Epidemiologic Research by Karolinska Institutet, celebrating his pioneering work in large-scale cohort studies on chronic diseases. <sup>[<sup>[[20]]()</sup>]()</sup> Hofman has received several honorary doctorates for his global influence in clinical epidemiology, including from the University of Belgrade in 2013, the University of Bern in 2020, and the National and Kapodistrian University of Athens in 2024. <sup>[<sup>[[6]]()</sup>]()</sup> <sup>[<sup>[[21]]()</sup>]()</sup> <sup>[<sup>[[22]]()</sup>]()</sup> In 2021, he was knighted as a Knight of the Order of the Lion of the Netherlands by King Willem-Alexander, in recognition of his lifelong dedication to advancing public health through epidemiological innovation. <sup>[<sup>[[1]]()</sup>]()</sup> More recently, in 2024, Hofman was appointed as the Bayer Fellow in Health & Biotech at the American Academy in Berlin, where he contributes to interdisciplinary discussions on health and biotechnology. <sup>[<sup>[[5]]()</sup>]()</sup>

### Key Publications and Editorial Influence

Albert Hofman has authored or co-authored over 2,500 publications in peer-reviewed journals, spanning topics in clinical epidemiology, genetics, and public health. <sup>[<sup>[[23]]()</sup>]()</sup> His prolific output includes several high-impact papers that have advanced understanding of chronic diseases. Notable examples encompass the 1997 _Lancet_ study linking atherosclerosis and apolipoprotein E to the prevalence of dementia and Alzheimer's disease, which highlighted vascular risk factors in neurodegeneration. <sup>[<sup>[[24]]()</sup>]()</sup> In 1998, Hofman contributed to a _New England Journal of Medicine_ (NEJM) article demonstrating associations between collagen type Iα1 gene alleles and reduced bone density, informing genetic predispositions to osteoporosis. <sup>[<sup>[[25]]()</sup>]()</sup> Further influential works include the 2001 NEJM paper on nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) and reduced risk of Alzheimer's disease, based on prospective cohort data, and the 2009 NEJM genome-wide association study (GWAS) identifying stroke susceptibility loci on chromosome 12p13. <sup>[<sup>[[26]]()</sup>]()</sup> <sup>[<sup>[[27]]()</sup>]()</sup> A 2013 _Science_ publication co-authored by Hofman reported GWAS findings from over 126,000 individuals, revealing genetic variants associated with educational attainment and their polygenic architecture. <sup>[<sup>[[28]]()</sup>]()</sup> Hofman's scholarly influence is reflected in citation metrics, with more than 84,000 total citations and an h-index of 267, underscoring the enduring impact of his contributions on epidemiological research. <sup>[<sup>[[23]]()</sup>]()</sup> Since 2003, Hofman has served as Editor-in-Chief of the _European Journal of Epidemiology_ , guiding its editorial direction toward rigorous, clinically relevant epidemiologic inquiry. <sup>[<sup>[[8]]()</sup>]()</sup> Under his leadership, the journal has implemented a hybrid open access model, enabling wider accessibility to articles while maintaining subscription-based options, which has supported global dissemination of research findings. <sup>[<sup>[[9]]()</sup>]()</sup> It has also emphasized applications in clinical medicine, public health, and preventive strategies, fostering publications on methodological innovations and empirical studies with direct translational potential. <sup>[<sup>[[9]]()</sup>]()</sup>
